q: What is abiotic synthesis in the context of the origin of life?
What type of membrane transport moves substances AGAINST their concentration gradient (from low to high concentration) and requires ATP?
What is the proportion of a specific allele in the gene pool called — and how does the sum of all such proportions at a locus compare to 1.0?
How does allopatric speciation differ from sympatric speciation?
a: Allopatric = geographic isolation; sympatric = same area

q: What type of enzyme regulation involves a molecule binding a non-active site to induce a conformational change that activates or inhibits?
What type of cell signaling occurs when a cell secretes a chemical signal that binds to receptors on that same cell, regulating its own activity?
What genetic engineering technique introduces foreign DNA (such as a recombinant plasmid) into a bacterial cell so it can replicate and express the new genes?
In a DNA double helix, what are the complementary base pairing rules — which nitrogenous base pairs with adenine, and which pairs with guanine?
Which theory, developed by Sutton and Boveri (~1902), states that genes are located on chromosomes and that chromosome behavior during meiosis explains Mendel's laws?
What is a coenzyme, and how does it differ from an inorganic cofactor?
What is a cofactor in enzyme function, and what distinguishes it from an organic coenzyme?
Which X-linked recessive trait affecting red-green color discrimination is more common in males than females because males need only one recessive allele to be affected?
What type of enzyme inhibition involves an inhibitor binding the active site and competing with substrate, overcome by adding more substrate?
Hox genes are structurally similar in all animals, and human and chimpanzee cytochrome c differ by only 1 amino acid. These are examples of what type of evidence?

Dolphins (mammals) and sharks (fish) have similar streamlined body shapes despite having very different evolutionary histories. What term describes the independent evolution of similar features in unrelated lineages experiencing similar selective pressures?
The widespread use of an organochlorine pesticide in the 20th century led to rapid evolution of resistance in mosquitoes and other insect populations. What is this a classic example of?
What type of cell communication occurs when membrane-bound signaling molecules on one cell bind directly to receptor proteins on an adjacent cell, requiring physical contact between cells?

After a forest fire, a diverse ecosystem with many species recovers within years, while a monoculture corn field requires human intervention to restore. What property allows diverse ecosystems to bounce back from disturbances?
What term describes the combined driving force of concentration gradient and electrical charge difference across a membrane that determines the direction of ion movement?
How do early vertebrate embryos provide evidence of shared ancestry?
What are non-superimposable molecules that are exact reflections of each other called, and which form does biology preferentially use for most compounds?
Which type of cell signaling releases hormones into the bloodstream to travel long distances and act on distant target cells?

One piece of evidence for the endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts is that both organelles have a _____, consistent with the outer membrane being derived from the host cell's engulfment vesicle.
In many reptiles, the sex of offspring is determined not by sex chromosomes but by the incubation temperature of the egg. What is this phenomenon called?
What type of fermentation converts pyruvate into ethanol and CO2 — the process used by yeast in bread-making and brewing?
What model, proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972, describes the plasma membrane as a fluid phospholipid bilayer with a mosaic of embedded proteins, cholesterol, and glycoproteins?
What functional distinction exists between free ribosomes in the cytoplasm and ribosomes bound to the rough ER?
a: Free ribosomes make cytoplasmic proteins; bound ribosomes make secretory and membrane proteins

q: How do functional groups determine the chemical properties of biological molecules such as reactivity, polarity, and solubility?
What phase of the cell cycle describes a state in which a cell has exited the active cycle, is no longer dividing, but can potentially re-enter the cycle if the right signals are received?
Which cell cycle checkpoint is considered the primary commitment point — sometimes called the restriction point — at which the cell decides whether to proceed with DNA replication or exit to G0?
Darwin observed that different species of these birds on the Galápagos Islands had beak shapes adapted to different food sources — which organisms were they?
Why is combination therapy (HAART) used to treat HIV instead of a single antiviral drug?
When humans sweat, water evaporating from the skin carries away large amounts of heat. Which property of water makes this evaporative cooling mechanism effective for maintaining body temperature?
What is the key difference between incomplete dominance and codominance in heterozygotes?
a: Incomplete dominance blends phenotypes; codominance expresses both simultaneously

q: What type of behavior is genetically programmed and performed correctly the first time, without prior experience?
During eukaryotic RNA processing, what happens to introns and exons in the pre-mRNA transcript?
Which enantiomeric form of protein monomers do ribosomes on Earth exclusively incorporate?
Which prokaryotic operon is a classic example of an inducible system, turned ON in the presence of lactose and OFF when glucose is the preferred energy source?
During DNA replication, which strand is synthesized continuously toward the replication fork, and which is synthesized discontinuously in short fragments away from the fork?
What term describes genes located on the same chromosome that tend to be inherited together and violate Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment?
A population initially grows rapidly, then slows as it approaches the environmental carrying capacity, eventually leveling off. What shape does this logistic growth curve produce?
What is mechanical isolation as a reproductive barrier?
During which phase of meiosis II do individual chromosomes (each consisting of two sister chromatids) align at the metaphase plate before sister chromatids are separated?
Which inheritance pattern involves organelle DNA passed exclusively through the egg cytoplasm from mothers to ALL offspring — bypassing Mendelian segregation?

The AP Biology CED states that which type of data is typically more accurate and reliable than morphological traits for constructing phylogenetic trees, because convergent evolution can make unrelated organisms look superficially similar?
How does a monophyletic clade differ from a paraphyletic group?
a: Monophyletic = ALL descendants; paraphyletic = SOME only

q: Which experiment crossed white-eyed male Drosophila with red-eyed females, finding that F2 white eyes appeared ONLY in males — proving the eye-color gene is on the X chromosome?
MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is a classic AP Biology example of what process?
What term describes the situation where more than two alleles exist in a population for a single gene, as in the ABO blood type system with I^A, I^B, and i?
Which active transport pump moves 3 Na⁺ out of and 2 K⁺ into the cell per ATP hydrolyzed, maintaining the resting membrane potential in animal cells?
What type of enzyme inhibition involves binding an allosteric site, changing enzyme shape, and cannot be overcome by adding more substrate?
How do the outcomes of nondisjunction in meiosis I differ from nondisjunction in meiosis II in terms of aneuploid gametes produced?
a: Meiosis I: all 4 gametes aneuploid; meiosis II: 2 of 4 gametes aneuploid

q: Genomic sequencing of populations over time, continuous change documented in the fossil record, antibiotic resistance, and emergent diseases all provide evidence for what AP Biology principle — that evolution is not a past event but a present and continuing process?
How does a paraphyletic group differ from a monophyletic group (clade)?
a: Paraphyletic = ancestor + SOME descendants; clade = ALL

q: Small polar uncharged molecules like water (H2O) and ammonia (NH3) can cross the plasma membrane _____, despite the hydrophobic interior creating a partial barrier to them.

Farmers who apply the same herbicide to their fields year after year often find that weed populations become resistant within a decade. What evolutionary phenomenon does this illustrate, alongside antibiotic resistance, as cited by the AP Biology CED?
What is the primary function of a phosphorylation cascade in signal transduction — what does it accomplish for the original signal?
Which photosystem splits water molecules to replace electrons lost when chlorophyll absorbs light — and releases O2 as a byproduct?
What is a phylogenetic tree in evolutionary biology?
In plant cells, what structure — formed by Golgi vesicles fusing at the cell's equator — serves as the new cell wall boundary that divides the cytoplasm during cytokinesis?
Which inheritance pattern, where two or more genes each contribute additively to a single trait, produces continuous phenotypic variation (e.g., skin color, height)?
What are postzygotic barriers in speciation?
How does punctuated equilibrium differ from phyletic gradualism?
a: Long stasis + rapid bursts of change; vs. slow gradualism

q: What highly selective type of endocytosis involves ligands binding to membrane receptor proteins, clustering in coated pits, and invaginating to form a coated vesicle — used for LDL cholesterol uptake?
Which type of allele is masked by the dominant allele in a heterozygote and is only expressed in the homozygous recessive (bb) condition?
What are chromosomes called that carry new allele combinations created by crossing over — combinations not present in either original parental chromosome?
How do meiosis I and meiosis II differ in terms of 'reductional' vs. 'equational' division?
a: Meiosis I is reductional; meiosis II is equational

q: What class of bacterial enzyme cuts double-stranded DNA at specific short palindromic sequences, producing fragments that are used in cloning and recombinant DNA technology?
During which phase of interphase does DNA replicate, producing two sister chromatids joined at a centromere for each chromosome?
What is a lipid chain with no carbon-carbon double bonds that packs tightly into a solid at room temperature, such as butter?
How do directional, stabilizing, and disruptive selection differ in their effects on a population's phenotype distribution?
a: Directional shifts mean; stabilizing reduces variance; disruptive increases variance

q: What term describes a trait that evolved in the common ancestor of a group and is shared by all descendants of that ancestor, used to identify clades and reconstruct phylogenetic trees?

Which condition illustrates how molecular variation in hemoglobin provides a fitness advantage in malaria-endemic environments, with heterozygotes gaining partial protection against malaria while avoiding severe anemia?
What are sister taxa in phylogenetics?
Which property of water allows it to absorb or release large amounts of heat with only small changes in temperature, helping living organisms maintain homeostatic body temperature?
What does the spindle assembly checkpoint (M checkpoint) verify before allowing the cell to proceed from metaphase to anaphase?
What is sympatric speciation and what makes it common in plants?
a: Same-area speciation; plants speciate via polyploidy

q: What genetic cross involves mating an individual of unknown genotype with a homozygous recessive individual (bb) to determine if the unknown is BB or Bb?

Which law of thermodynamics explains why living systems require a continuous input of energy — because disorder (entropy) in the universe tends to increase, and maintaining biological order requires energy input?
According to AP Biology CED, what are the three mechanisms by which sexual reproduction generates genetic variation among offspring?
Tiktaalik (~375 mya) is a transitional fossil that bridges which two major groups?
In what direction does RNA polymerase read the DNA template strand, and in what direction is the mRNA strand synthesized?
After a ligand binds to the extracellular domain of a receptor protein, what structural change occurs in the receptor to begin the transduction phase?

When sea otters were hunted to near-extinction off the Pacific coast, sea urchin populations exploded and consumed kelp forests, which collapsed — destroying habitat for hundreds of species. This chain reaction of effects across trophic levels is called what?
Which prokaryotic operon is a classic example of a repressible system, switched OFF when tryptophan — the end product of the pathway — is abundant?
Which genome-editing system uses a single guide RNA (sgRNA) to direct the Cas9 nuclease to make a precise double-strand break at a target DNA sequence in virtually any genome?
A double bond in a fatty acid's carbon chain causes the chain to kink, preventing tight packing and resulting in a lipid that is liquid at room temperature. This describes which type of fatty acid?
What are vestigial structures, and what is their evolutionary significance?
What type of lipid made of a long-chain alcohol and a fatty chain serves as a waterproofing agent in plant cuticles and feathers?
Whales have small, non-functional pelvic bones buried deep in their body. These are an example of what type of structure?
Electrons in filled inner shells that do not participate in chemical bonding, shield valence electrons from nuclear charge, and appear in a noble gas shorthand bracket notation are called what?
Moving left to right across a period on the periodic table, what happens to atomic radius?
The net positive charge experienced by an electron after accounting for the shielding effect of inner electrons is called what?
Moving down a group on the periodic table, what happens to metallic character?
In chromatography, components of a mixture are separated based on differences in what?
a: differences in polarity or affinity for the stationary phase

q: When balancing a chemical equation, which numbers are you ALLOWED to change to make atom counts equal on both sides?
To identify the limiting reagent when given moles of each reactant, you divide each reactant's available moles by its stoichiometric coefficient. The limiting reagent is which?
a: the reactant with the smaller mole-to-coefficient ratio

q: What experimental technique is used to determine reaction orders by comparing how doubling one reactant's concentration affects the initial rate?
What term describes the slowest elementary step in a reaction mechanism, which controls the overall rate of the reaction?
On a potential energy diagram, the horizontal axis is called the ___ and represents the progress of the reaction from reactants to products.
In an ICE table, what information is entered in each of the three rows — I, C, and E — when solving an equilibrium problem from initial conditions?
When a weak acid reacts completely with a strong base, the resulting salt solution is ___.
Which hydrogen halide is classified as a weak acid despite fluorine being the most electronegative halogen?
When the number of moles of gas increases during a chemical reaction, the entropy of the system generally ___.
An electrochemical cell in which both electrodes are made of the same material but are in contact with solutions of different ion concentrations is called a ___.
Some ionic compounds dissolve spontaneously even though dissolution is endothermic (ΔH > 0). The thermodynamic driving force in these cases is a large increase in ___.
In brass, zinc atoms replace some copper atoms in the crystal lattice because the atoms are similar in size. What type of alloy is brass?
When choosing among multiple valid Lewis structures for the same molecule, the preferred (most accurate) structure is the one that does what with formal charges?
A carbon atom in ethylene (C2H4) has three electron domains — two C–H bonds and one C=C bond. What hybridization does this carbon adopt?
A double bond between two atoms consists of how many sigma bonds and how many pi bonds?
a: One sigma bond and one pi bond

q: What specifically Florentine form of Renaissance thought held that educated citizens had a duty to participate actively in the governance and civic life of their community?
What term identifies the brief peak period of Italian Renaissance art (c.1490–1527) associated with Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Raphael?
What series of conflicts (1494–1559) saw France, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire compete for dominance over the Italian peninsula?
Which French bishop served as tutor to the Dauphin and wrote Politics Drawn from the Very Words of Holy Scripture, the definitive theological defense of absolute monarchy?
What 18th-century European intellectual movement, also known by its French name Lumières, championed reason, science, and human progress over tradition and religious authority?
What 1637 work by Descartes introduced his method of systematic doubt and the rationalist approach to knowledge, famously containing the phrase "I think, therefore I am"?
Which 1830 Eugène Delacroix work depicted a woman personifying Liberty guiding armed citizens over barricades and became an icon of revolutionary Romantic art?
Giuseppe Mazzini was called the ___ of Italian unification for his republican ideals and inspiration of the Young Italy movement.
Which two vast multi-ethnic polities — the Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian — were most threatened by 19th-century nationalist movements?

On a PPC showing the trade-off between two types of goods, producing more of which type today — capital investment or consumer goods — leads to faster economic growth and an outward shift of the future PPC?
What is the name of the curve that shows tax revenue as a function of tax rate, illustrating that revenue first rises then falls as tax rates approach 100%?
What type of money has no intrinsic value and is declared legal tender by government decree, such as U.S. paper dollars today?
What phenomenon describes the tendency for budget deficits and current account (trade) deficits to occur together in the same country?
A negative income elasticity of demand (YED < 0) identifies a good as which type, meaning demand falls as income rises?
What term describes a cost that has already been paid and cannot be recovered, regardless of future production decisions?
If an object moves in the positive direction but its acceleration points in the negative direction, what is happening to its speed?
a: Opposite to the direction of motion (object is slowing down)

q: What angle of an incline causes an object to just begin sliding, and how is it related to the coefficient of static friction?
a: The angle of repose θ where tan θ = μs

q: What rotational analog of acceleration measures the rate of change of angular velocity, in units of rad/s²?
What rotational analog of displacement measures the angle through which an object rotates, typically in radians?
What is the rotational analog of the impulse-momentum theorem?
a: Angular impulse = τ × Δt = ΔL (rotational analog of impulse-momentum theorem)

q: What do 'at rest' and 'moving at constant velocity' have in common regarding acceleration?
a: At rest and moving at constant velocity both have zero acceleration

q: For an Atwood machine (two unequal masses m₁ < m₂ connected over a frictionless pulley), what is the system's acceleration?
In an Atwood machine with a massless rope and ideal frictionless pulley, what is true about the rope tension?
a: The tension is the same throughout a massless rope over an ideal pulley

q: At the peak of a vertically thrown ball's trajectory, what are its velocity and acceleration?
a: v = 0 at the peak; acceleration = g downward throughout

q: On a banked curve with no friction, what force provides the centripetal acceleration for a car?
a: The horizontal component of the normal force provides centripetal force

q: What are three real-world applications of the Bernoulli effect (faster flow = lower pressure)?
a: Higher speed flow has lower pressure (Bernoulli): airplane wings, Venturi tubes, curved ball spin

q: Why is 'centrifugal force' (the outward force felt in circular motion) not a real force in AP Physics 1?
a: Centrifugal force is a fictitious force

q: Why is 'centripetal force' never drawn as a separate arrow on a free-body diagram?
a: Centripetal force is NOT a separate force

q: A student draws a free-body diagram for a car on a circular track and adds a 'centripetal force' arrow. What is wrong?
a: Centripetal force is not a separate force

q: What is the relationship between the period T and frequency f of circular motion?
For an object moving in a circular path with fixed centripetal force and mass, how does speed change if the radius doubles?
a: At fixed centripetal force: speed increases if radius increases (v² = Fr/m)

q: During a collision between two objects with no external horizontal forces, what happens to the velocity of the center of mass?
a: The center of mass velocity stays constant throughout a collision if no external forces act

q: What value of the coefficient of restitution (e = relative speed after/before collision) characterizes an elastic vs. perfectly inelastic collision?
a: Coefficient of restitution (e): e=1 elastic, e=0 perfectly inelastic

q: A dying star collapses into a neutron star, reducing its radius by a factor of 100,000. What happens to its rotation rate?
a: A collapsing star spins faster as its radius shrinks, conserving angular momentum

q: When adding two vectors using components, what is the correct procedure?
a: Add x-components together and y-components together separately

q: Under what condition is the angular momentum of a system conserved?
Water flows through a pipe that narrows to half its original diameter. By what factor does the flow speed change?
a: Where a pipe narrows, flow speed increases (A₁v₁ = A₂v₂); narrower = faster

q: What happens to the amplitude of oscillation when a spring-mass system experiences friction?
a: Amplitude decreases over time due to friction or air resistance (energy lost to heat)

q: A solid disk and a hollow ring of equal mass and radius roll down an incline. Which reaches the bottom first?
a: Solid disk (I = ½MR²) reaches bottom faster; less rotational inertia than hollow ring (I = MR²)

q: Which quantity measures total path length traveled and is a scalar (not a vector)?
Qualitatively, how does air drag affect a falling object, and what is terminal velocity?
a: Drag increases with speed and opposes motion; terminal velocity when drag equals weight

q: What is the formula for efficiency of an energy conversion process?
a: Efficiency = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%

q: What distinguishes an elastic collision from an inelastic collision?
a: Both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved in elastic collisions

q: What does an energy bar chart (LOL diagram) show in AP Physics 1?
a: Bar charts show KE + PE + W_friction = constant total energy at each stage

q: What are the four main forms of energy tracked in AP Physics 1 conservation problems?
a: Kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, and thermal energy in AP Physics 1

q: What happens in a perfectly elastic 1D collision between two equal-mass balls when one is stationary?
a: Moving object stops; stationary target moves at the original speed (1D elastic, equal masses)

q: What is the distinction between contact forces and field forces, and which appear on free-body diagrams?
a: Contact forces (normal, friction, tension) and field forces (gravity, electric); both appear on FBDs

q: What forces should appear on a free-body diagram of a single object?
a: All forces acting ON the object (not forces the object exerts on others)

q: Why does a figure skater spin faster when they pull their arms in?
a: Pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω (L = Iω = constant since no external torque)

q: What equation gives the volume flow rate Q (volume per unit time) through a pipe in terms of cross-sectional area A and fluid speed v?
How does Newton's Third Law explain why a submerged object experiences a buoyant force?
a: The fluid pushes back with an equal and opposite force (Newton's Third Law)

q: Why can Newton's 3rd law force pairs never cancel each other out on a free-body diagram?
a: Newton's 3rd law force pairs always act on DIFFERENT objects

q: Does the kinetic friction force change if you increase the contact area between two surfaces (same material, same normal force)?
a: Kinetic friction does not depend on the contact area between surfaces

q: A tire gauge reads 30 PSI (gauge). What is the actual (absolute) pressure inside the tire?
a: Absolute pressure = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure; gauge is measured relative to atmosphere

q: Why can you choose any reference level for gravitational potential energy?
a: Gravitational PE = mgh; h is measured from any chosen reference level

q: How does the gravitational force between two objects change if the distance between them is doubled?
a: Gravity follows an inverse-square law with distance

q: When is it appropriate to use F_g = mg vs. F_g = Gm₁m₂/r² for gravitational force?
a: Near-surface: F_g = mg; Universal: F_g = Gm₁m₂/r²

q: Using conservation of energy, what is the speed of an object that falls from height h starting from rest?
a: v = √(2gh) for an object falling from height h from rest (ignoring friction)

q: What does it mean that the spring force is a 'restoring force' in Hooke's Law?
a: The spring force is proportional to and opposite the displacement from equilibrium

q: For a projectile launched at the same initial speed, at what launch angle is horizontal range maximized (on level ground)?
a: Range increases as launch angle approaches 45°

q: A narrow cylinder and a wide tank are both filled with water to the same depth. Which has greater pressure at the bottom?
a: Pressure at a given depth depends only on depth and fluid density, not the shape of the container

q: Why can we usually ignore external forces like gravity or friction during a collision when applying momentum conservation?
a: During a brief collision, external forces are negligible compared to the large collision forces

q: What does the area under a Force-versus-time graph represent?
What theorem states that the impulse delivered to an object equals its change in momentum?
For an object on a frictionless incline of angle θ, how do you decompose the weight into components?
a: Component along incline = mg sinθ; component perpendicular = mg cosθ

q: For an object on an incline at angle θ, what are the components of gravity parallel and perpendicular to the incline surface?
a: F_g parallel = mg sin θ; F_g perpendicular = mg cos θ

q: What type of collision conserves momentum but NOT kinetic energy, with some KE converted to other forms?
What type of reference frame moves at constant velocity (zero acceleration), so Newton's laws hold without modification?
In a perfectly inelastic collision, what is the easiest method to find kinetic energy lost?
a: ΔKE_lost = ½(m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂)) × (v₁ − v₂)²  for head-on perfectly inelastic collision

q: In an inelastic collision, where does the 'lost' kinetic energy go?
a: Some kinetic energy converts to thermal energy, sound, or deformation

q: If a car's speed doubles, by what factor does its kinetic energy change?
a: Doubling speed quadruples kinetic energy (KE ∝ v²)

q: If a satellite's orbital radius doubles, by what factor does its orbital period increase?
a: T² ∝ r³: if orbital radius doubles, period increases by a factor of 2√2 ≈ 2.83

q: Which kinematic equation gives position as a function of time for constant acceleration (includes a ½at² term)?
What is the difference between a barometer and a manometer?
a: Barometer measures atmospheric pressure; manometer measures gauge pressure of a gas

q: What is the key distinction between mass and weight?
a: Mass is amount of matter (kg); weight is gravitational force (N)

q: What are the moments of inertia for a solid disk, a hollow ring, and a solid sphere (all about their symmetry axis)?
a: Solid disk: I = ½MR²; hollow ring: I = MR²; solid sphere: I = (2/5)MR²; rod (center): I = (1/12)ML²

q: In an elastic collision, what quantities are conserved?
a: Both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved

q: Under what condition is the total momentum of a system conserved?
a: Total momentum of a system is constant only when the net external force on the system is zero

q: Does negative acceleration always mean an object is slowing down?
What is the directional relationship between the net force on an object and its acceleration?
a: The net force and the acceleration always point in the same direction

q: What is neutral buoyancy and how do submarines achieve it?
a: Neutral buoyancy: object's average density exactly equals fluid density; buoyant force equals weight

q: On a potential energy diagram, what do PE minima and maxima tell you about equilibrium?
a: Stable equilibrium at a potential energy minimum; unstable equilibrium at a maximum

q: Does the work done by gravity depend on whether a ball rolls down a ramp or falls straight down the same vertical distance?
a: Only the vertical height change matters, not the path taken

q: Which type of collision results in the greatest loss of kinetic energy?
a: A perfectly inelastic collision (objects stick together) has maximum kinetic energy loss

q: A pendulum clock is moved to a location with double the usual swing amplitude. Does it keep accurate time?
a: Period is independent of amplitude for ideal springs and small-angle pendulums

q: How does the amplitude of oscillation affect the period of a simple harmonic oscillator?
a: Period is independent of amplitude in SHM

q: What is the strategic advantage of choosing a smart pivot point when writing the torque equation for static equilibrium?
a: Choose pivot where unknown force acts

q: What is the key distinction between position and displacement?
What does power measure, and how is it related to force and velocity?
a: Power is the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = Fv (in Watts = J/s)

q: What is the pressure at depth h in a fluid of density ρ with surface pressure P₀?
a: P = P₀ + ρgh (absolute pressure at depth h = surface pressure plus ρgh)

q: A bullet is fired from a stationary gun at rest. Why does the gun recoil backward?
a: The gun recoils backward with the same magnitude momentum as the bullet (but opposite direction)

q: If object A moves at velocity vA and object B moves at velocity vB (both relative to ground), what is the velocity of A as seen by B?
a: Velocity of object A relative to B equals vA minus vB

q: What condition relates the center-of-mass velocity to the angular velocity for an object rolling without slipping?
What is the total kinetic energy of an object rolling without slipping?
a: Rolling KE = ½mv² + ½Iω² (translation + rotation); for solid disk: = ¾mv²

q: How are the rotational kinematic equations related to the linear kinematic equations?
a: Rotational equations mirror linear ones: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ

q: How do the rotational kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration relate to the linear kinematic equations?
a: Rotational equations mirror linear equations (θ↔x, ω↔v, α↔a)

q: For an orbiting satellite, what equation expresses the balance between gravitational force and the centripetal force requirement?
a: Gravitational force provides centripetal force: Gm₁m₂/r² = mv²/r

q: What is the orbital speed of a satellite at orbital radius r around a planet of mass M?
a: Orbital speed v = √(GM/r); faster for lower orbits (closer to planet)

q: A heavy person sits 1 m from the pivot of a seesaw; where must a lighter person sit to balance it?
a: m₁ × d₁ = m₂ × d₂ (torques balance about the pivot when in equilibrium)

q: In simple harmonic motion, where does the oscillating object have maximum acceleration?
In simple harmonic motion, at what position does the oscillating object have maximum speed?
What mathematical condition must the restoring force satisfy for an object to undergo Simple Harmonic Motion?
a: A restoring force proportional to displacement from equilibrium: F = −kx

q: Ranking from fastest to slowest rolling down a ramp: solid sphere, hollow sphere, solid cylinder, hollow cylinder. What is the order?
a: Solid sphere rolls faster than hollow sphere; smaller I means more translational KE

q: What quantity in Hooke's Law measures how stiff a spring is, with SI units of N/m?
How does doubling the mass or doubling the spring constant each affect the period of a spring-mass oscillator?
a: Increasing mass increases period; increasing spring constant decreases period (T = 2π√(m/k))

q: How does the effective spring constant differ for springs connected in series vs. in parallel?
a: Springs in series: k_eff = k₁k₂/(k₁+k₂); springs in parallel: k_eff = k₁ + k₂

q: What is the key difference between static friction and kinetic friction?
a: Static friction is greater than kinetic friction; static prevents sliding, kinetic opposes sliding

q: For a projectile launched and landing at the same height, how do the launch and landing speeds compare?
a: The projectile returns to the same height with the same speed but opposite vertical velocity

q: In systems thinking, what is the difference between internal and external forces?
a: Internal forces act between objects within the system; external forces act from outside

q: When is it valid to treat a multi-object system as a single object in Newton's 2nd law?
a: When all objects have the same acceleration, treat them as one combined mass

q: For an ideal (massless, frictionless) pulley system with a single rope, how does tension vary along the rope?
a: Tension is the same throughout a massless, frictionless rope

q: Under what condition is the tension in a rope uniform (the same at every point)?
a: Tension varies along a massive rope; it is uniform only in a massless (ideal) rope

q: What is the lever arm in a torque calculation, and why does it use perpendicular distance?
a: Lever arm is the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation to the line of action of the force

q: What is the formula for work done by a constant torque rotating an object through angle Δθ?
a: W = τ × Δθ (rotational work equals torque times angular displacement in radians)

q: Under what conditions does a force produce zero torque about a given axis?
a: Torque is zero when force is parallel to position vector r (θ = 0° or 180°) or when r = 0

q: What is total mechanical energy and when is it conserved?
a: Total mechanical energy E = KE + PE; conserved when only conservative forces do work

q: What graphical method for adding vectors involves placing the tail of the second vector at the tip of the first?
Vector subtraction A − B is equivalent to what operation?
a: Adding the negative of the second vector

q: What common misconception about velocity and acceleration does AP Physics 1 specifically address?
a: Velocity and acceleration can point in different directions

q: What is viscosity, and what distinguishes laminar from turbulent flow?
a: Viscosity is resistance to flow; laminar = smooth layers; turbulent = chaotic eddies

q: An astronaut travels from Earth to the Moon. What happens to their mass and weight?
